content.text: I’m going to add a small tutorial plus a local demo script. The script will create disposable source/target repos, serve them through Git’s smart HTTP backend, run `plan`, `sync`, branch mapping, and an incremental update, then show the resulting refs.

*** Add File: docs/demo-local-smart-http.sh
+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+set -euo pipefail
+
+ROOT="${GITSYNC_DEMO_ROOT:-$(mktemp -d)}"
+SERVER_GO="$ROOT/git-http-demo-server.go"
+URL_FILE="$ROOT/server-url.txt"
+LOG_FILE="$ROOT/server.log"
+
+cleanup() {
+  if [[ -n "${SERVER_PID:-}" ]]; then
+    kill "$SERVER_PID" >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
+  fi
+  if [[ -z "${GITSYNC_DEMO_ROOT:-}" ]]; then
+    rm -rf "$ROOT"
+  else
+    printf 'kept demo root: %s\n' "$ROOT"
+  fi
+}
+trap cleanup EXIT
+
+run() {
+  printf '\n$ %s\n' "$*"
+  "$@"
+}
+
+write_server() {
+  cat >"$SERVER_GO" <<'GO'
+package main
+
+import (
+	"fmt"
+	"log"
+	"net"
+	"net/http"
+	"net/http/cgi"
+	"os"
+	"os/exec"
+)
+
+func main() {
+	if len(os.Args) != 3 {
+		log.Fatalf("usage: %s <repo-root> <url-file>", os.Args[0])
+	}
+	repoRoot := os.Args[1]
+	urlFile := os.Args[2]
+
+	gitPath, err := exec.LookPath("git")
+	if err != nil {
+		log.Fatal(err)
+	}
+
+	handler := &cgi.Handler{
+		Path: gitPath,
+		Args: []string{"http-backend"},
+		Env: []string{
+			"GIT_PROJECT_ROOT=" + repoRoot,
+			"GIT_HTTP_EXPORT_ALL=1",
+		},
+	}
+
+	listener, err := net.Listen("tcp", "127.0.0.1:0")
+	if err != nil {
+		log.Fatal(err)
+	}
+	baseURL := "http://" + listener.Addr().String()
+	if err := os.WriteFile(urlFile, []byte(baseURL), 0o644); err != nil {
+		log.Fatal(err)
+	}
+	fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "serving %s at %s\n", repoRoot, baseURL)
+	log.Fatal(http.Serve(listener, handler))
+}
+GO
+}
+
+setup_repos() {
+  run git init --bare "$ROOT/source.git"
+  run git init --bare "$ROOT/target.git"
+  run git -C "$ROOT/target.git" config http.receivepack true
+
+  run git init -b main "$ROOT/work"
+  run git -C "$ROOT/work" config user.name "git-sync demo"
+  run git -C "$ROOT/work" config user.email "git-sync@example.com"
+  printf 'hello from source\n' >"$ROOT/work/README.md"
+  run git -C "$ROOT/work" add README.md
+  run git -C "$ROOT/work" commit -m "initial commit"
+  run git -C "$ROOT/work" remote add origin "$ROOT/source.git"
+  run git -C "$ROOT/work" push origin main
+}
+
+wait_for_server() {
+  for _ in {1..100}; do
+    if [[ -s "$URL_FILE" ]]; then
+      cat "$URL_FILE"
+      return
+    fi
+    sleep 0.1
+  done
+  printf 'server did not start; log follows:\n' >&2
+  cat "$LOG_FILE" >&2 || true
+  exit 1
+}
+
+write_server
+setup_repos
+
+run mise exec -- go build -o "$ROOT/git-sync" ./cmd/git-sync
+
+run mise exec -- go run "$SERVER_GO" "$ROOT" "$URL_FILE" >"$LOG_FILE" 2>&1 &
+SERVER_PID=$!
+BASE_URL="$(wait_for_server)"
+SOURCE_URL="$BASE_URL/source.git"
+TARGET_URL="$BASE_URL/target.git"
+
+printf '\nsource: %s\n' "$SOURCE_URL"
+printf 'target: %s\n' "$TARGET_URL"
+
+run "$ROOT/git-sync" plan --branch main --json "$SOURCE_URL" "$TARGET_URL"
+run "$ROOT/git-sync" sync --branch main --json "$SOURCE_URL" "$TARGET_URL"
+
+printf '\nTarget refs after initial sync:\n'
+run git --git-dir="$ROOT/target.git" show-ref
+
+printf '\nAdd a second source commit, then sync incrementally:\n'
+printf 'second line\n' >>"$ROOT/work/README.md"
+run git -C "$ROOT/work" add README.md
+run git -C "$ROOT/work" commit -m "second commit"
+run git -C "$ROOT/work" push origin main
+run "$ROOT/git-sync" sync --branch main --stats --json "$SOURCE_URL" "$TARGET_URL"
+
+printf '\nMap source main to target stable:\n'
+run "$ROOT/git-sync" sync --map main:stable --json "$SOURCE_URL" "$TARGET_URL"
+
+printf '\nFinal target refs:\n'
+run git --git-dir="$ROOT/target.git" show-ref
+
+printf '\nDemo completed successfully.\n'
*** Add File: docs/tutorial.md
+# git-sync Tutorial
+
+This project is a Go command-line tool and library for mirroring Git refs from one remote to another without creating a local checkout. The main command is `git-sync`; the benchmark helper is `git-sync-bench`.
+
+## 1. Set Up The Toolchain
+
+The repo uses `mise` to install Go and the project tools.
+
+```bash
+cd /Users/rizel/Documents/work/gitsync
+mise install
+mise exec -- go version
+```
+
+You can run commands through `mise exec -- ...`, or activate mise in your shell and run `go` directly.
+
+## 2. Verify The Project
+
+Run the default test suite:
+
+```bash
+mise run test
+```
+
+Build the CLI:
+
+```bash
+mise run build
+```
+
+That writes a `git-sync` binary in the repo root. You can also skip the binary and run directly:
+
+```bash
+mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync --help
+```
+
+Note: the current CLI prints help as an error and exits non-zero for `--help`, but the usage text is still the right reference.
+
+## 3. The Main Commands
+
+Use `plan` when you want to preview what would happen:
+
+```bash
+mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync plan --json <source-url> <target-url>
+```
+
+Use `sync` for the normal workflow. It bootstraps an empty target and handles later fast-forward updates:
+
+```bash
+mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync sync <source-url> <target-url>
+```
+
+Use `replicate` when the source should overwrite the target through relay-only execution. If relay is not possible, it fails instead of falling back to local materialization:
+
+```bash
+mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync replicate --stats <source-url> <target-url>
+```
+
+Use `probe` to inspect refs and remote capabilities:
+
+```bash
+mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync probe --tags --json <source-url> <target-url>
+```
+
+Use `fetch` for lower-level source fetch experiments:
+
+```bash
+mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync fetch --branch main --json <source-url>
+```
+
+Important flag placement: put flags before positional URLs. For example:
+
+```bash
+mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync sync --branch main --json <source-url> <target-url>
+```
+
+## 4. Local Demo: Smart HTTP Source And Target
+
+The main CLI talks Git smart HTTP, so a plain local `file://` URL is not enough for `git-sync sync`. This repo includes a disposable local demo script that creates two bare repos, serves them through `git http-backend`, and runs real CLI commands against them.
+
+Run it from the repo root:
+
+```bash
+bash docs/demo-local-smart-http.sh
+```
+
+The script demonstrates:
+
+- `plan --branch main --json`
+- first `sync` into an empty target
+- an incremental `sync` after a second source commit
+- `sync --map main:stable` to copy a source branch into a differently named target branch
+- verification with `git show-ref` inside the bare target
+
+By default, the script deletes its temporary files. To keep the demo repos for inspection:
+
+```bash
+GITSYNC_DEMO_ROOT=/tmp/gitsync-demo bash docs/demo-local-smart-http.sh
+```
+
+Then inspect the result:
+
+```bash
+git --git-dir=/tmp/gitsync-demo/source.git show-ref
+git --git-dir=/tmp/gitsync-demo/target.git show-ref
+git --git-dir=/tmp/gitsync-demo/target.git log --oneline --all --decorate
+```
+
+## 5. Hosted Remote Example
+
+For GitHub or another hosted Git provider, pass HTTPS clone URLs. Tokens can be passed as flags:
+
+```bash
+mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
+  --source-token "$GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN" \
+  --target-token "$GITSYNC_TARGET_TOKEN" \
+  --branch main \
+  --json \
+  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
+  https://github.com/target-org/target-repo.git
+```
+
+Or set environment variables:
+
+```bash
+export GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN=...
+export GITSYNC_TARGET_TOKEN=...
+export GITSYNC_SOURCE_USERNAME=git
+export GITSYNC_TARGET_USERNAME=git
+
+mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
+  --branch main \
+  https://github.com/source-org/source-repo.git \
+  https://github.com/target-org/target-repo.git
+```
+
+If your normal Git credential helper knows the remotes, `git-sync` can also use that instead of explicit token flags.
+
+## 6. Common Demo Scenarios
+
+Mirror only selected branches:
+
+```bash
+mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
+  --branch main,release \
+  <source-url> \
+  <target-url>
+```
+
+Mirror tags too:
+
+```bash
+mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
+  --branch main \
+  --tags \
+  <source-url> \
+  <target-url>
+```
+
+Map a source branch to a different target branch:
+
+```bash
+mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
+  --map main:stable \
+  <source-url> \
+  <target-url>
+```
+
+Preview a replicate run:
+
+```bash
+mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync plan \
+  --mode replicate \
+  --branch main \
+  --json \
+  <source-url> \
+  <target-url>
+```
+
+Collect transfer statistics and memory measurements:
+
+```bash
+mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
+  --stats \
+  --measure-memory \
+  --json \
+  <source-url> \
+  <target-url>
+```
+
+## 7. Benchmark Demo
+
+Build the benchmark helper:
+
+```bash
+mise exec -- go build -o /tmp/git-sync-bench ./cmd/git-sync-bench
+```
+
+Run a small local benchmark against a local source repo path:
+
+```bash
+/tmp/git-sync-bench \
+  --scenario bootstrap \
+  --source-url /tmp/gitsync-demo/source.git \
+  --repeat 3 \
+  --branch main \
+  --stats \
+  --json
+```
+
+For larger real-world tests, use a local mirror as the source rather than repeatedly benchmarking directly against a hosted service.
+
+## 8. How To Read The Output
+
+In text mode, look for counts like `pushed`, `skipped`, `blocked`, and `deleted`.
+
+In JSON mode, the most useful fields are:
+
+- `plans`: per-ref planned actions
+- `pushed`: refs actually updated
+- `skipped`: refs that were already current
+- `blocked`: refs that could not update safely
+- `deleted`: refs pruned from the target
+- `relay` and `relayMode`: whether the operation used relay, such as `bootstrap` or incremental relay
+- `stats`: request, byte, want, have, and push-command counters when `--stats` is set
+- `measurement`: elapsed time and heap data when `--measure-memory` is set
+
+If `sync` reports blocked refs, the target has diverged or the requested update is not fast-forwardable. Use `plan --json` to inspect the reason, then decide whether to fix the target, change the ref mapping, or rerun with `--force`.
+
+## 9. Where To Look Next
+
+- `README.md`: command overview and auth behavior
+- `docs/architecture.md`: when this tool fits and how relay/materialized paths work
+- `docs/protocol.md`: smart HTTP protocol details
+- `docs/testing.md`: default, integration, smoke, and benchmark test commands
+- `example_test.go`: minimal library API example
*** End Patch

content.text: The first local demo exposed a real macOS/CGI edge case: chunked request bodies are rejected by `git http-backend` when run through CGI. I’m tightening the tiny demo server so it buffers request bodies and sets `Content-Length`, which keeps the demo simple and still exercises real smart HTTP.

 import (

 	"log"

 	"net/http"
@@
-	baseURL := "http://" + listener.Addr().String()
+	baseURL := "http://" + listener.Addr().String()
 	if err := os.WriteFile(urlFile, []byte(baseURL), 0o644); err != nil {
 		log.Fatal(err)
 	}
 	fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "serving %s at %s\n", repoRoot, baseURL)
-	log.Fatal(http.Serve(listener, handler))
+	log.Fatal(http.Serve(listener, http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
+		if r.Body != nil {
+			body, err := io.ReadAll(r.Body)
+			if err != nil {
+				http.Error(w, err.Error(), http.StatusBadRequest)
+				return
+			}
+			_ = r.Body.Close()
+			r.Body = io.NopCloser(bytes.NewReader(body))
+			r.ContentLength = int64(len(body))
+			r.Header.Set("Content-Length", fmt.Sprintf("%d", len(body)))
+		}
+		handler.ServeHTTP(w, r)
+	})))
 }

Your URLs are just the HTTPS clone URLs for the GitHub repos:

Example shape:

```bash
SOURCE_URL="https://github.com/entire-dev/some-existing-repo.git"
TARGET_URL="https://github.com/entire-dev/git-sync-demo-target.git"
```

```text
git-sync-demo-target
```

```bash
export GITSYNC_SOURCE_TOKEN=...
export GITSYNC_TARGET_TOKEN=...

mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync plan \
  --branch main \
  --json \
  "$SOURCE_URL" \
```

Then the actual sync:

```bash
mise exec -- go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
```

content.text:
Yes, and I made that easier.

You can now run the demo in a screenshot/recording-friendly mode:

```bash
cd /Users/rizel/Documents/work/gitsync
GITSYNC_DEMO_SANITIZE_OUTPUT=1 bash docs/demo-local-smart-http.sh
```

It still runs against real disposable local repos, but the terminal shows clean fake URLs like:

```bash
git-sync plan --branch main --json https://github.com/acme/source-repo.git https://github.com/acme/target-repo.git
```

So for the screenshot, you can capture the `plan` output without exposing any real source/target:

```json
"action": "create",
"dryRun": true,
"sourceRef": "refs/heads/main",
"targetRef": "refs/heads/main"
```

You can customize the fake names too:

```bash
GITSYNC_DEMO_SANITIZE_OUTPUT=1 \
GITSYNC_DEMO_DISPLAY_SOURCE_URL=https://github.com/entire-dev/source.git \
GITSYNC_DEMO_DISPLAY_TARGET_URL=https://github.com/entire-dev/new-home.git \
bash docs/demo-local-smart-http.sh
```

That’s probably the cleanest path for your tweet assets: real terminal, real `git-sync`, fake repo URLs.

+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+set -euo pipefail
+
+ROOT="${GITSYNC_DEMO_ROOT:-$(mktemp -d)}"
+SERVER_GO="$ROOT/git-http-demo-server.go"
+URL_FILE="$ROOT/server-url.txt"
+LOG_FILE="$ROOT/server.log"
+SOURCE_ALIAS="${GITSYNC_DEMO_SOURCE_ALIAS:-former-host/former-repo}"
+TARGET_ALIAS="${GITSYNC_DEMO_TARGET_ALIAS:-new-host/new-repo}"
+
+cleanup() {
+  if [[ -n "${SERVER_PID:-}" ]]; then
+    kill "$SERVER_PID" >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
+    wait "$SERVER_PID" 2>/dev/null || true
+  fi
+  if [[ -z "${GITSYNC_DEMO_ROOT:-}" ]]; then
+    rm -rf "$ROOT"
+  else
+    printf 'kept demo root: %s\n' "$ROOT"
+  fi
+}
+trap cleanup EXIT
+
+write_server() {
+  cat >"$SERVER_GO" <<'GO'
+package main
+
+import (
+	"bytes"
+	"fmt"
+	"io"
+	"log"
+	"net"
+	"net/http"
+	"net/http/cgi"
+	"os"
+	"os/exec"
+)
+
+func main() {
+	if len(os.Args) != 3 {
+		log.Fatalf("usage: %s <repo-root> <url-file>", os.Args[0])
+	}
+	repoRoot := os.Args[1]
+	urlFile := os.Args[2]
+
+	gitPath, err := exec.LookPath("git")
+	if err != nil {
+		log.Fatal(err)
+	}
+
+	handler := &cgi.Handler{
+		Path: gitPath,
+		Args: []string{"http-backend"},
+		Env: []string{
+			"GIT_PROJECT_ROOT=" + repoRoot,
+			"GIT_HTTP_EXPORT_ALL=1",
+		},
+	}
+
+	listener, err := net.Listen("tcp", "127.0.0.1:0")
+	if err != nil {
+		log.Fatal(err)
+	}
+	baseURL := "http://" + listener.Addr().String()
+	if err := os.WriteFile(urlFile, []byte(baseURL), 0o644); err != nil {
+		log.Fatal(err)
+	}
+	log.Fatal(http.Serve(listener, http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
+		if r.Body != nil {
+			body, err := io.ReadAll(r.Body)
+			if err != nil {
+				http.Error(w, err.Error(), http.StatusBadRequest)
+				return
+			}
+			_ = r.Body.Close()
+			r.Body = io.NopCloser(bytes.NewReader(body))
+			r.ContentLength = int64(len(body))
+			r.TransferEncoding = nil
+			r.Header.Set("Content-Length", fmt.Sprintf("%d", len(body)))
+		}
+		handler.ServeHTTP(w, r)
+	})))
+}
+GO
+}
+
+wait_for_server() {
+  for _ in {1..100}; do
+    if [[ -s "$URL_FILE" ]]; then
+      cat "$URL_FILE"
+      return
+    fi
+    sleep 0.1
+  done
+  printf 'server did not start; log follows:\n' >&2
+  cat "$LOG_FILE" >&2 || true
+  exit 1
+}
+
+setup_repos() {
+  git init --bare "$ROOT/source.git" >/dev/null
+  git init --bare "$ROOT/target.git" >/dev/null
+  git -C "$ROOT/target.git" config http.receivepack true
+
+  git init -b main "$ROOT/work" >/dev/null
+  git -C "$ROOT/work" config user.name "git-sync demo"
+  git -C "$ROOT/work" config user.email "git-sync@example.com"
+  printf 'hello from source\n' >"$ROOT/work/README.md"
+  git -C "$ROOT/work" add README.md
+  git -C "$ROOT/work" commit -m "initial commit" >/dev/null
+  git -C "$ROOT/work" remote add origin "$ROOT/source.git"
+  git -C "$ROOT/work" push origin main >/dev/null
+}
+
+write_wrapper() {
+  cat >"$ROOT/git-sync" <<EOF
+#!/usr/bin/env bash
+set -euo pipefail
+
+args=()
+for arg in "\$@"; do
+  case "\$arg" in
+    "$SOURCE_ALIAS")
+      args+=("$SOURCE_URL")
+      ;;
+    "$TARGET_ALIAS")
+      args+=("$TARGET_URL")
+      ;;
+    *)
+      args+=("\$arg")
+      ;;
+  esac
+done
+
+exec "$ROOT/git-sync-real" "\${args[@]}"
+EOF
+  chmod +x "$ROOT/git-sync"
+}
+
+setup_repos
+mise exec -- go build -o "$ROOT/git-sync-real" ./cmd/git-sync
+
+write_server
+mise exec -- go run "$SERVER_GO" "$ROOT" "$URL_FILE" >"$LOG_FILE" 2>&1 &
+SERVER_PID=$!
+BASE_URL="$(wait_for_server)"
+SOURCE_URL="$BASE_URL/source.git"
+TARGET_URL="$BASE_URL/target.git"
+write_wrapper
+
+cat <<EOF
+Demo shell ready.
+
+You can type these commands exactly:
+
+  ./git-sync plan --branch main --json $SOURCE_ALIAS $TARGET_ALIAS
+  ./git-sync sync --branch main --json $SOURCE_ALIAS $TARGET_ALIAS
+
+To demo an incremental sync, run this setup command first:
+
+  printf 'second line\n' >> work/README.md && git -C work add README.md && git -C work commit -m 'second commit' && git -C work push origin main
+
+Then type:
+
+  ./git-sync sync --branch main --stats --json $SOURCE_ALIAS $TARGET_ALIAS
+  ./git-sync sync --map main:stable --json $SOURCE_ALIAS $TARGET_ALIAS
+  git --git-dir=target.git show-ref
+
+Temporary demo directory:
+  $ROOT
+
+Type exit when done.
+EOF
+
+cd "$ROOT"
+exec "${SHELL:-/bin/sh}"
*** End Patch

why does it name the folder  tmp.4M8311LTc3 is that what would happen in real life

No, that temp folder name is only from the demo setup.

The script creates disposable local repos with `mktemp`, so macOS gives it a random directory like:

```text
/tmp/tmp.4M8311LTc3
```

That is not a `git-sync` thing, and it would not appear in normal real-life usage.

In real usage you’d run something like:

```bash
git-sync plan --branch main --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
```

or with real remote URLs:

```bash
git-sync plan --branch main --json https://old-host/org/repo.git https://new-host/org/repo.git
```

The temporary folder only exists so the demo can create fake source/target repos locally without touching any real host. It is basically the backstage area. Your screenshot/recording should focus on the command and JSON output, not the “Temporary demo directory” line.


rizel@Rizels-MacBook-Pro git-sync-recording-demo % git-sync plan --branch main --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
error: sync: plan: list source refs: request info-refs: Get "http://127.0.0.1:63083/former-repo.git/info/refs?service=git-upload-pack": round trip: dial tcp 127.0.0.1:63083: connect: connection refused

That error means you’re in an old `/tmp/git-sync-recording-demo` directory after the demo server stopped. The fake command maps to a real localhost server, and that server has to be running.

I updated [docs/demo-recording-shell.sh](/Users/rizel/Documents/work/gitsync/docs/demo-recording-shell.sh) to make the server more stable by building it as a real binary instead of running it through `go run`.

Start fresh:

```bash
cd /Users/rizel/Documents/work/gitsync
bash docs/demo-recording-shell.sh
```

Then, inside the shell it opens, type:

```bash
git-sync plan --branch main --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
```

Important bit: don’t open a separate terminal and run it from `/tmp/git-sync-recording-demo`; use the shell that the script drops you into. When you type `exit`, the demo server is done.
alright..what would be the next command to run

Next run the actual sync:

```bash
git-sync sync --branch main --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
```

For the screen recording, highlight these fields in the output:

```json
"pushed": 1,
"relay": true,
"relayMode": "bootstrap"
```

Then verify the new repo got the branch:

```bash
git --git-dir=new-repo.git show-ref
```
what about probe or something else

Yes, `probe` is a good “before plan” or “after setup” command, especially for screenshots. It shows git-sync can inspect the remotes before deciding what to do.

Run:

```bash
git-sync probe --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
```

If you want tags included:

```bash
git-sync probe --tags --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
```

For the demo sequence, I’d do:

```bash
git-sync probe --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
git-sync plan --branch main --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
git-sync sync --branch main --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
git --git-dir=new-repo.git show-ref
```

For tweet/video value:

- `probe`: “can inspect source + target”
- `plan`: “can preview exact ref updates”
- `sync`: “can execute via bootstrap relay”
- `show-ref`: “target really changed”

If you only have 30-60 seconds, I’d skip `probe` in the video and use it only if you want an extra screenshot. `plan` is more valuable visually.
if i run sync..i wont be able to run it a second time right?

You can run it a second time. It just won’t push anything if nothing changed.

After the first sync, run the same command again:

```bash
git-sync sync --branch main --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
```

You should see something like:

```json
"pushed": 0,
"skipped": 1
```

That’s actually a useful demo beat: rerunning is safe/idempotent when the target is already up to date.

Then, to make a more exciting second sync, add a new commit to the former repo:

```bash
printf 'second line\n' >> former-repo-worktree/README.md
git -C former-repo-worktree add README.md
git -C former-repo-worktree commit -m 'second commit'
git -C former-repo-worktree push origin main
```

Now run sync again:

```bash
git-sync sync --branch main --stats --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
```

That one should show:

```json
"action": "update",
"pushed": 1,
"relay": true,
"relayMode": "incremental",
"stats": {
  "enabled": true
}
```
yeah but it wont do the same results right? so i gotta do them separately

Right. Once you run `sync`, the demo state changes.

So for clean screenshots/recording, treat them as separate beats:

1. **Before sync**
   Run:
   ```bash
   git-sync probe --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
   git-sync plan --branch main --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
   ```
   This shows empty target / planned create / `dryRun: true`.

2. **First sync**
   Run:
   ```bash
   git-sync sync --branch main --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
   ```
   This shows:
   ```json
   "pushed": 1,
   "relayMode": "bootstrap"
   ```

3. **Second immediate sync**
   Run:
   ```bash
   git-sync sync --branch main --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
   ```
   This shows:
   ```json
   "pushed": 0,
   "skipped": 1
   ```

4. **Incremental update**
   Add a new commit, then sync:
   ```bash
   printf 'second line\n' >> former-repo-worktree/README.md
   git -C former-repo-worktree add README.md
   git -C former-repo-worktree commit -m 'second commit'
   git -C former-repo-worktree push origin main

   git-sync sync --branch main --stats --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
   ```
   This shows:
   ```json
   "action": "update",
   "pushed": 1,
   "relayMode": "incremental"
   ```

If you want the exact first-sync output again, restart the demo shell:

```bash
exit
cd /Users/rizel/Documents/work/gitsync
bash docs/demo-recording-shell.sh
```
oh okay..i would just need to restart the demo shell for this? to be able to run sync?

Exactly. Restarting the demo shell resets the whole local demo back to the beginning.

Do this:

```bash
exit
cd /Users/rizel/Documents/work/gitsync
bash docs/demo-recording-shell.sh
```

Then you’re back to a fresh state where `new-repo` is empty again, so this will show the first-sync result:

```bash
git-sync sync --branch main --json former-host/former-repo new-host/new-repo
```

Expected highlights:

```json
"pushed": 1,
"relay": true,
"relayMode": "bootstrap"
```

So your workflow can be:

- Restart shell → capture `plan`
- Restart shell → capture first `sync`
- Restart shell → record the full flow, if needed

The demo is disposable, so restarting is totally fine.

```text
Most git migration workflows turn remote-to-remote movement into a local storage problem:

git-sync is built around a different model: inspect remotes, plan ref updates, then stream the packfile across when it can.
```

does this run in the background at all

No. `git-sync` itself does **not** run in the background.

It’s a command you run, it does the sync, prints output, and exits. So for the thread, describe it as something you can put into automation, jobs, scripts, CI, migration workflows, etc., but not as a background daemon.

In our demo shell, there *is* a tiny local HTTP server running in the background, but that’s only for the fake local recording setup. Real `git-sync` usage is just:

```bash
git-sync plan ...
git-sync sync ...
```

Run → output → exit.
